romania.title: Pacient în România. Cum e salvată o pacientă care a suferit un un atac vascular cerebral: la fiecare 4 minute, un român suferă un AVC
romania.summary: La fiecare câteva minute, un român suferă un AVC. Diferența dintre o viață normală și o dizabilitate permanentă sau chiar deces se măsoară doar în minute. Procedura de îndepărtare a cheagului, care poate duce la moartea creierului, trebuie făcută cât mai rapid și doar în centre specializate.

romania.title: Chirurgia robotică, șansa unei femei cu cancer rectal metastatic. Medic: „Evităm colostomele în mai mult de jumătate din cazuri”
romania.summary: Există viaţă şi după un diagnostic de cancer cu metastaze, iar o femeie de 52 de ani din Sibiu a primit o nouă şansă după o intervenţie chirurgicală complexă realizată cu ajutorul robotului da Vinci, de o echipă medicală din cadrul Spitalului MedLife Polisano. Graţie preciziei robotului şi a expertizei medicilor, pentru femeia care suferea de cancer de rect cu invazie în organele din jur a fost găsită soluţia salvatoare, fără a fi nevoie de colostomie (anus contra naturii).

Aceştia dau năvală în pet-shopuri în căutare de tigri, după ce copiii lor s-au uitat la „Cămătarii” şi au rămas fermecaţi de simpaticele pufoşenii cu dinţi ascuţiţi şi gheare care te pot eviscera într-o secundă. „Am făcut prostia să-i pun ‘Cămătarii’ pe tabletă […] The post Pet-shopuri pline de părinţi disperaţi: copiii au văzut „Cămătarii” şi acum vor şi ei tigru appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Rural patients at high risk for obstructive sleep apnea less likely to be referred for evaluation than urban patients
medical.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that patients at high risk for obstructive sleep apnea who receive primary care in rural settings are less likely to be referred for sleep apnea evaluation than those in urban areas, even after accounting for individual and neighborhood socioeconomic factors.

medical.title: New map reveals how antidepressants reshape the brain's serotonin system
medical.summary: A new study has uncovered how antidepressants affect different groups of serotonin-producing brain cells in opposite ways, offering new insights into why selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) can cause unpleasant side effects at the start of treatment but lead to symptom relief over time.

medical.title: Novel disorder causing severe respiratory dysfunction linked to loss-of-function gene variant
medical.summary: A new report in the American Journal of Human Genetics describes a novel disorder caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in the TMEM63B gene, which results in severe lung disease. Researchers at Baylor College of Medicine, Texas Children's Hospital and collaborating institutions in Asia and Europe explain how this disorder presented in five individuals from four unrelated families.

medical.title: AI can reveal brain tumor risks without costly genetic testing
medical.summary: Mayo Clinic researchers and collaborators have shown that artificial intelligence (AI) can analyze routine pathology slides to help classify meningiomas, the most common primary brain tumor in adults, and predict a patient's risk of tumor recurrence.

medical.title: Africa races for Ebola vaccine as outbreak outpaces response
medical.summary: Researchers racing to develop a vaccine to fight the growing Ebola outbreak centered in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) say doses could be ready for human testing within "two to three months," while the more promising candidate could take up to nine months.

medical.title: Researchers trigger sleep's restorative effect in parts of the awake brain
medical.summary: By inducing specific patterns of activity in small portions of the brain in awake mice, researchers have triggered a recalibration of neural connections that normally only occurs during sleep. This new approach offset the effects of sleep deprivation on memory tasks and revealed features of sleep that are key to its restorative effect.

medical.title: Trial to test AI for prostate MRI cancer diagnosis
medical.summary: A new UCL-led international trial will assess whether AI can perform as well as expert radiologists at detecting prostate cancer on MRI scans.

medical.title: Music listening shows no mental health benefit in 20,000 twin records
medical.summary: For many, music is a source of comfort, a mood booster and a remedy for loneliness. But does frequently listening to music actually lead to better mental health? A new study by researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Empirical Aesthetics (MPIEA) in Frankfurt am Main, Germany, shows that the connection is not as clear-cut. The results were recently published in Journal of Affective Disorders Reports.

medical.title: Delirium after surgery is a strong predictor of cognitive decline in older adults, finds study
medical.summary: Researchers found postoperative delirium was strongly associated with long-term cognitive decline and the effect was not explained by rehospitalizations, highlighting the long-term impact of delirium on brain health.

medical.title: Testing AI against public health's existing tools shows mixed results
medical.summary: A new Penn-led randomized controlled trial has found that AI-powered chatbots can make vaccine-hesitant parents more likely to say they will immunize their children against human papillomavirus (HPV), but no more than standard written public health materials.

medical.title: Ozempic, GPL-1s may help curb substance use disorders, study finds
medical.summary: A new study led by researchers at the University of Texas at El Paso found that use of weight-loss drugs like Ozempic and other GLP-1s is associated with a lower risk of developing alcohol, opioid, nicotine and cocaine use disorders.

medical.title: Can effectiveness of memory processing be predicted with a saliva test?
medical.summary: A study led by ICREA researcher Raül Andero at the Institut de Neurociències of the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (INc-UAB) has found that the relationship between a person's progesterone and estradiol levels at a given moment, measured in saliva, could help predict participants' performance in a learning and memory task.

medical.title: Outdoor play at ages two to four linked to better mental health by age eight
medical.summary: Children who spend more time playing outdoors between the ages of 2 and 4 may be less likely to develop emotional and behavioral difficulties later in childhood. That's according to new research led by the University of Exeter, published in the Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry.

medical.title: Childhood of short sleep doubles risk of teenage depression
medical.summary: Children who experience persistent sleep issues throughout their younger years may have a greater risk of developing depression in adolescence, a new study suggests.

medical.title: How often do people pass gas? There's now an app for that
medical.summary: Flatulence, or farting, is something people often joke about or find embarrassing when it happens unexpectedly. It is, however, an essential bodily function that allows the digestive system to keep pressure within the intestinal tract low and prevents painful stretching of the stomach and intestines. Even though it is normal to fart, it remains unclear what counts as a healthy number.

medical.title: The Commonwealth Games relay taking aim at ocean plastic
medical.summary: A new campaign is using sport’s global reach to stop one million pieces of plastic reaching seas and rivers The post The Commonwealth Games relay taking aim at ocean plastic appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
medical.summary: Europe’s rivers flowed more freely, scientists hailed a new pill for pancreatic cancer, and a ‘lost species' was found, plus more The post What went right this week: the good news that matters appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: My morning at a sober breakfast rave – and what I got out of it
medical.summary: A new ecstatic dance craze fuses spirituality, physicality and fun. Sarah LaBrecque joined 100 ravers one Sunday morning The post My morning at a sober breakfast rave – and what I got out of it appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Scientists discover hidden driver of aging — Simple supplement reversed brain decline
medical.summary: A newly identified brain protein may play a major role in how the body ages. Researchers discovered that declining levels of Menin in the hypothalamus triggered inflammation, memory problems, bone loss, and other aging-related changes in mice. Restoring Menin reversed several of these effects, while a simple amino acid supplement called D-serine boosted cognition. The discovery opens a surprising new path for fighting age-related decline.

science.title: Mobile money can fight poverty, but trust is vital
science.summary: Mobile money can help people without bank accounts take part in the economy, but trust and fairness will ultimately determine whether it succeeds, a new study has found.

science.title: Researchers craft a new, simple recipe for highly entangled quantum states
science.summary: Building useful quantum technologies—from sensors to computers—requires generating highly complex entangled states, in which the properties of particles are deeply intertwined. Producing such states has traditionally required complex tools and carefully engineered setups with many parts.

science.title: The path to teen radicalization isn't as straight as people think
science.summary: Amid growing public concern about the radicalization of young people, there is a widespread misconception that radicalization logically and inevitably progresses to violence.

science.title: Lighter X-ray aprons could spare health care workers from chronic pain
science.summary: A light, flexible polymer material developed at the University of Waterloo could replace the lead in heavy X-ray aprons, providing the same protection from harmful radiation while reducing their weight by almost 90%.

science.title: Why plant cells need heme: Hidden signal reshapes photosynthesis gene control
science.summary: For plants, light is an important environmental factor not only as a source of energy for photosynthesis, but also as a signal for capturing environmental information. Light signals are sensed by photoreceptor proteins called phytochromes. The amino acids that make up proteins are transparent and cannot absorb visible light. Therefore, pigments are essential for photoreceptor proteins to sense light, and these are called chromophores.

science.title: Yeast experiments reveal an evolutionarily conserved backup route for making a molecule that's essential to life
science.summary: Hiroshima University researchers say a newly proposed three-step "detour" pathway for making dolichol, a molecule cells need to properly process proteins, may be more universal than scientists realized. Experiments in yeast suggest eukaryotes may rely on overlapping biochemical pathways, including the evolutionarily conserved detour and evidence of a possible backup route, to produce a molecule essential to life.

science.title: Why does the Y chromosome retain UTY?
science.summary: A study, published in the journal Development, is the first to precisely map endogenous UTY occupancy across the human genome and demonstrate that UTY remains functionally involved in transcriptional regulation during early human development.

science.title: A 170 km journey by a freshwater stingray challenges long-held conservation assumptions
science.summary: Most sharks and rays inhabit the oceans, but a small proportion thrive in freshwater. Approximately 4% to 5% of all shark and ray species live in tropical and subtropical rivers around the world. Two main ecological strategies can be distinguished among freshwater sharks and rays.

science.title: Luxury brands turn memes into more shares, clicks and laughs across four experiments
science.summary: Luxury brands have increasingly embraced internet memes in their social media advertising campaigns. For example, Gucci's #TFWGucci campaign demonstrated how luxury brands can incorporate memes into their digital communication strategies to capture consumers' attention. Memes—digital content that spreads rapidly through sharing, imitation and adaptation across online communities—have become a popular way for brands to engage consumers.

science.title: Black hole feeding bursts may explain JWST's Little Red Dots in early universe
science.summary: A new theoretical study may have cracked one of the most puzzling discoveries of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST): Little Red Dots, spotted across the early universe. The paper, posted to the arXiv preprint server on May 29, argues that these objects could be black holes caught in rare, violent bursts of feeding at a rate exceeding theoretical limits.

science.title: New evidence from Yinshan Block reveals Earth's early supercontinent cycles
science.summary: A new study published in Precambrian Research by Jawad Shabbir, a Ph.D. student at Peking University's School of Earth and Space Sciences under Professor Song Shuguang, addresses a critical yet poorly understood period in Earth history. The Archean–Proterozoic transition witnessed global tectonic evolution, cratonization, glaciation, banded iron formations and the Great Oxygenation Event—events linked to supercontinent formation. Focusing on the Yinshan Block within the North China Craton (NCC), the research challenges previous ambiguities by revealing two complete orogenic cycles driven by tectonic processes similar to those on modern Earth.

science.title: Gleam-glum effect reveals emotional word cues in children as young as five
science.summary: The words "tick-tock," "hiss" and "screech" are examples of onomatopoeia because they imitate the sounds they represent: the rhythmic ticking of a clock; an angry cat, or a slowly deflating bike tire; a high-pitched scream. Onomatopoeia is a type of sound symbolism.

science.title: Astronomers discover the earliest known flickering quasar
science.summary: A supermassive black hole lies at the heart of every galaxy, including the Milky Way. When a black hole is active, it pulls material in as a whirlpool of high-temperature gas and dust. As this cosmic material piles up and falls onto a black hole, it lights up its vicinity, radiating a huge amount of energy.

science.title: Secondary silylium ion drives one-pot ketone sulfonamidation, reaching 95% yields
science.summary: A research team has developed a novel organocatalysis method based on a silylium Lewis acid. This technology employs an ion-pair catalyst combining a diethylsilylium ion with a weakly coordinating anion, enabling the direct installation of sulfonamide groups into functionalized ketone compounds, including β-ketoesters, which had previously been difficult to react using conventional catalytic methods.

science.title: How Artemis II livestreamed hi-def videos and images from the moon to Earth
science.summary: This April, humanity had front-row seats to space as the Artemis II Orion spacecraft transmitted crystal-clear footage of its historic journey around the moon from more than 250,000 miles (about 402,000 kilometers) back to Earth at speeds on par with home internet connections.

science.title: Technology used to monitor conservation efforts at Rome's Colosseum to be used at the Ipiranga Museum
science.summary: The same three-dimensional laser scanning technology used to monitor the Colosseum in Rome will be used in a conservation project at the Ipiranga Museum in São Paulo, Brazil. Beatriz Kuhl, a professor at the University of São Paulo's Faculty of Architecture and Urbanism (FAU-USP), presented the initiative, which is scheduled to begin in July, during FAPESP Week London, held June 2–4.

science.title: How NFL draft position overpromises player potential
science.summary: A new analysis suggests there is no correlation between athlete performance and the way the NFL values draft positions, challenging long-held beliefs about how professional football teams select new players each year.

science.title: Alien signal claims face stricter verification under updated disclosure rules
science.summary: The IAA SETI Committee has updated rules for evaluating and revealing the detection of extraterrestrial intelligence.

science.title: These horses are unaffected by petting in children's zoo, heart rate monitors reveal
science.summary: The horses at the Children's Zoo in Gothenburg don't mind being petted by children and adults. However, they do get stressed by the noise from an excavator. Researchers at the University of Gothenburg discovered this after fitting heart rate monitors to eight Gotland Russ horses. The research is published in the journal Zoo Biology.

science.title: Bacteria can learn and form memories without a brain
science.summary: Researchers at Carnegie Mellon University have shown that bacteria can learn from past experiences, store memories across generations and adapt their behavior to changing environments, all without a brain or nervous system. The research could shape how scientists think about bacterial infections and antibiotic treatment.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: ZOB lansează dublul vinil „Greatest (H̷)Tits”, cu 23 de piese din discografia trupei
mix_items.summary: Pe 18 iunie 2026, trupa ZOB lansează la Quantic dublul vinil „Greatest (H̷)Tits”, în cadrul unui concert în care vor urca pe scenă și foștii membri Lonți, Forrest și Doru.… Articolul ZOB lansează dublul vinil „Greatest (H̷)Tits”, cu 23 de piese din discografia trupei apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Statele Unite doresc să expulzeze imigranți ilegali în una dintre cele mai sărace țări din lume, cu un PIB pe cap de locuitor mai mic decât al unor state măcinate de războaie civile, au declarat pentru Reuters două surse care afirmă că un acord în acest sens a fost convenit deja. Washingtonul a expulzat deja …

mix_items.title: UiPath România și-a crescut veniturile, dar a ieșit pe pierderi în anul 2025. Mai mulți angajați
mix_items.summary: Subsidiara din România a companiei AI româno-americane UiPath a raportat venituri în creștere pe anul 2025, dar cheltuielile i-au crescut și mai mult, firma bucureșteană trecând astfel pe pierdere netă, potrivit datelor oficiale de la Ministerul Finanțelor, publicate recent.

mix_items.title: Chirurgia robotică, șansa unei femei cu cancer rectal metastatic. Medic: „Evităm colostomele în mai mult de jumătate din cazuri”
mix_items.summary: Există viaţă şi după un diagnostic de cancer cu metastaze, iar o femeie de 52 de ani din Sibiu a primit o nouă şansă după o intervenţie chirurgicală complexă realizată cu ajutorul robotului da Vinci, de o echipă medicală din cadrul Spitalului MedLife Polisano. Graţie preciziei robotului şi a expertizei medicilor, pentru femeia care suferea de cancer de rect cu invazie în organele din jur a fost găsită soluţia salvatoare, fără a fi nevoie de colostomie (anus contra naturii).

mix_items.title: Pacient în România. Cum e salvată o pacientă care a suferit un un atac vascular cerebral: la fiecare 4 minute, un român suferă un AVC
mix_items.summary: La fiecare câteva minute, un român suferă un AVC. Diferența dintre o viață normală și o dizabilitate permanentă sau chiar deces se măsoară doar în minute. Procedura de îndepărtare a cheagului, care poate duce la moartea creierului, trebuie făcută cât mai rapid și doar în centre specializate.

mix_items.title: 😂 Pet-shopuri pline de părinţi disperaţi: copiii au văzut „Cămătarii” şi acum vor şi ei tigru
mix_items.summary: Serialul-fenomen despre fraţii Cămătaru îi pune la încercare pe mii de părinţi! mix_items.summary: Măsuri sporite de siguranță la munte pentru conducătorii de ATV-uri. mix_items.title: Mobile money can fight poverty, but trust is vital
mix_items.summary: Mobile money can help people without bank accounts take part in the economy, but trust and fairness will ultimately determine whether it succeeds, a new study has found.

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Rural patients at high risk for obstructive sleep apnea less likely to be referred for evaluation than urban patients
mix_items.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that patients at high risk for obstructive sleep apnea who receive primary care in rural settings are less likely to be referred for sleep apnea evaluation than those in urban areas, even after accounting for individual and neighborhood socioeconomic factors.

mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: New map reveals how antidepressants reshape the brain's serotonin system
mix_items.summary: A new study has uncovered how antidepressants affect different groups of serotonin-producing brain cells in opposite ways, offering new insights into why selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) can cause unpleasant side effects at the start of treatment but lead to symptom relief over time.

mix_items.title: Researchers craft a new, simple recipe for highly entangled quantum states
mix_items.summary: Building useful quantum technologies—from sensors to computers—requires generating highly complex entangled states, in which the properties of particles are deeply intertwined. Producing such states has traditionally required complex tools and carefully engineered setups with many parts.

mix_items.summary: Amid growing public concern about the radicalization of young people, there is a widespread misconception that radicalization logically and inevitably progresses to violence.

mix_items.title: Novel disorder causing severe respiratory dysfunction linked to loss-of-function gene variant
mix_items.summary: A new report in the American Journal of Human Genetics describes a novel disorder caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in the TMEM63B gene, which results in severe lung disease. Researchers at Baylor College of Medicine, Texas Children's Hospital and collaborating institutions in Asia and Europe explain how this disorder presented in five individuals from four unrelated families.

mix_items.title: AI can reveal brain tumor risks without costly genetic testing
mix_items.summary: Mayo Clinic researchers and collaborators have shown that artificial intelligence (AI) can analyze routine pathology slides to help classify meningiomas, the most common primary brain tumor in adults, and predict a patient's risk of tumor recurrence.

mix_items.title: Lighter X-ray aprons could spare health care workers from chronic pain
mix_items.summary: A light, flexible polymer material developed at the University of Waterloo could replace the lead in heavy X-ray aprons, providing the same protection from harmful radiation while reducing their weight by almost 90%.

mix_items.title: Africa races for Ebola vaccine as outbreak outpaces response
mix_items.summary: Researchers racing to develop a vaccine to fight the growing Ebola outbreak centered in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) say doses could be ready for human testing within "two to three months," while the more promising candidate could take up to nine months.

mix_items.title: Why plant cells need heme: Hidden signal reshapes photosynthesis gene control
mix_items.summary: For plants, light is an important environmental factor not only as a source of energy for photosynthesis, but also as a signal for capturing environmental information. Light signals are sensed by photoreceptor proteins called phytochromes. The amino acids that make up proteins are transparent and cannot absorb visible light. Therefore, pigments are essential for photoreceptor proteins to sense light, and these are called chromophores.

mix_items.title: Yeast experiments reveal an evolutionarily conserved backup route for making a molecule that's essential to life
mix_items.summary: Hiroshima University researchers say a newly proposed three-step "detour" pathway for making dolichol, a molecule cells need to properly process proteins, may be more universal than scientists realized. Experiments in yeast suggest eukaryotes may rely on overlapping biochemical pathways, including the evolutionarily conserved detour and evidence of a possible backup route, to produce a molecule essential to life.

mix_items.title: Why does the Y chromosome retain UTY?
mix_items.summary: A study, published in the journal Development, is the first to precisely map endogenous UTY occupancy across the human genome and demonstrate that UTY remains functionally involved in transcriptional regulation during early human development.

mix_items.title: Researchers trigger sleep's restorative effect in parts of the awake brain
mix_items.summary: By inducing specific patterns of activity in small portions of the brain in awake mice, researchers have triggered a recalibration of neural connections that normally only occurs during sleep. This new approach offset the effects of sleep deprivation on memory tasks and revealed features of sleep that are key to its restorative effect.

mix_items.title: A 170 km journey by a freshwater stingray challenges long-held conservation assumptions
mix_items.summary: Most sharks and rays inhabit the oceans, but a small proportion thrive in freshwater. Approximately 4% to 5% of all shark and ray species live in tropical and subtropical rivers around the world. Two main ecological strategies can be distinguished among freshwater sharks and rays.

mix_items.title: Trial to test AI for prostate MRI cancer diagnosis
mix_items.summary: A new UCL-led international trial will assess whether AI can perform as well as expert radiologists at detecting prostate cancer on MRI scans.

mix_items.title: Luxury brands turn memes into more shares, clicks and laughs across four experiments
mix_items.summary: Luxury brands have increasingly embraced internet memes in their social media advertising campaigns. For example, Gucci's #TFWGucci campaign demonstrated how luxury brands can incorporate memes into their digital communication strategies to capture consumers' attention. Memes—digital content that spreads rapidly through sharing, imitation and adaptation across online communities—have become a popular way for brands to engage consumers.

mix_items.title: Black hole feeding bursts may explain JWST's Little Red Dots in early universe
mix_items.summary: A new theoretical study may have cracked one of the most puzzling discoveries of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST): Little Red Dots, spotted across the early universe. The paper, posted to the arXiv preprint server on May 29, argues that these objects could be black holes caught in rare, violent bursts of feeding at a rate exceeding theoretical limits.

mix_items.title: Music listening shows no mental health benefit in 20,000 twin records
mix_items.summary: For many, music is a source of comfort, a mood booster and a remedy for loneliness. But does frequently listening to music actually lead to better mental health? A new study by researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Empirical Aesthetics (MPIEA) in Frankfurt am Main, Germany, shows that the connection is not as clear-cut. The results were recently published in Journal of Affective Disorders Reports.

mix_items.title: New evidence from Yinshan Block reveals Earth's early supercontinent cycles
mix_items.summary: A new study published in Precambrian Research by Jawad Shabbir, a Ph.D. student at Peking University's School of Earth and Space Sciences under Professor Song Shuguang, addresses a critical yet poorly understood period in Earth history. The Archean–Proterozoic transition witnessed global tectonic evolution, cratonization, glaciation, banded iron formations and the Great Oxygenation Event—events linked to supercontinent formation. Focusing on the Yinshan Block within the North China Craton (NCC), the research challenges previous ambiguities by revealing two complete orogenic cycles driven by tectonic processes similar to those on modern Earth.

mix_items.title: Gleam-glum effect reveals emotional word cues in children as young as five
mix_items.summary: The words "tick-tock," "hiss" and "screech" are examples of onomatopoeia because they imitate the sounds they represent: the rhythmic ticking of a clock; an angry cat, or a slowly deflating bike tire; a high-pitched scream. Onomatopoeia is a type of sound symbolism.

mix_items.title: Astronomers discover the earliest known flickering quasar
mix_items.summary: A supermassive black hole lies at the heart of every galaxy, including the Milky Way. When a black hole is active, it pulls material in as a whirlpool of high-temperature gas and dust. As this cosmic material piles up and falls onto a black hole, it lights up its vicinity, radiating a huge amount of energy.

mix_items.title: Delirium after surgery is a strong predictor of cognitive decline in older adults, finds study
mix_items.summary: Researchers found postoperative delirium was strongly associated with long-term cognitive decline and the effect was not explained by rehospitalizations, highlighting the long-term impact of delirium on brain health.

mix_items.title: Testing AI against public health's existing tools shows mixed results
mix_items.summary: A new Penn-led randomized controlled trial has found that AI-powered chatbots can make vaccine-hesitant parents more likely to say they will immunize their children against human papillomavirus (HPV), but no more than standard written public health materials.

mix_items.title: Secondary silylium ion drives one-pot ketone sulfonamidation, reaching 95% yields
mix_items.summary: A research team has developed a novel organocatalysis method based on a silylium Lewis acid. This technology employs an ion-pair catalyst combining a diethylsilylium ion with a weakly coordinating anion, enabling the direct installation of sulfonamide groups into functionalized ketone compounds, including β-ketoesters, which had previously been difficult to react using conventional catalytic methods.

mix_items.title: Ozempic, GPL-1s may help curb substance use disorders, study finds
mix_items.summary: A new study led by researchers at the University of Texas at El Paso found that use of weight-loss drugs like Ozempic and other GLP-1s is associated with a lower risk of developing alcohol, opioid, nicotine and cocaine use disorders.

mix_items.title: Can effectiveness of memory processing be predicted with a saliva test?
mix_items.summary: A study led by ICREA researcher Raül Andero at the Institut de Neurociències of the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (INc-UAB) has found that the relationship between a person's progesterone and estradiol levels at a given moment, measured in saliva, could help predict participants' performance in a learning and memory task.

mix_items.title: How Artemis II livestreamed hi-def videos and images from the moon to Earth
mix_items.summary: This April, humanity had front-row seats to space as the Artemis II Orion spacecraft transmitted crystal-clear footage of its historic journey around the moon from more than 250,000 miles (about 402,000 kilometers) back to Earth at speeds on par with home internet connections.

mix_items.title: Technology used to monitor conservation efforts at Rome's Colosseum to be used at the Ipiranga Museum
mix_items.summary: The same three-dimensional laser scanning technology used to monitor the Colosseum in Rome will be used in a conservation project at the Ipiranga Museum in São Paulo, Brazil. Beatriz Kuhl, a professor at the University of São Paulo's Faculty of Architecture and Urbanism (FAU-USP), presented the initiative, which is scheduled to begin in July, during FAPESP Week London, held June 2–4.

mix_items.title: Outdoor play at ages two to four linked to better mental health by age eight
mix_items.summary: Children who spend more time playing outdoors between the ages of 2 and 4 may be less likely to develop emotional and behavioral difficulties later in childhood. That's according to new research led by the University of Exeter, published in the Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry.

mix_items.title: How NFL draft position overpromises player potential
mix_items.summary: A new analysis suggests there is no correlation between athlete performance and the way the NFL values draft positions, challenging long-held beliefs about how professional football teams select new players each year.

mix_items.title: Childhood of short sleep doubles risk of teenage depression
mix_items.summary: Children who experience persistent sleep issues throughout their younger years may have a greater risk of developing depression in adolescence, a new study suggests.

mix_items.title: How often do people pass gas? There's now an app for that
mix_items.summary: Flatulence, or farting, is something people often joke about or find embarrassing when it happens unexpectedly. It is, however, an essential bodily function that allows the digestive system to keep pressure within the intestinal tract low and prevents painful stretching of the stomach and intestines. Even though it is normal to fart, it remains unclear what counts as a healthy number.

mix_items.title: Alien signal claims face stricter verification under updated disclosure rules
mix_items.summary: The IAA SETI Committee has updated rules for evaluating and revealing the detection of extraterrestrial intelligence.

mix_items.title: These horses are unaffected by petting in children's zoo, heart rate monitors reveal
mix_items.summary: The horses at the Children's Zoo in Gothenburg don't mind being petted by children and adults. However, they do get stressed by the noise from an excavator. Researchers at the University of Gothenburg discovered this after fitting heart rate monitors to eight Gotland Russ horses. The research is published in the journal Zoo Biology.

mix_items.title: Bacteria can learn and form memories without a brain
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Carnegie Mellon University have shown that bacteria can learn from past experiences, store memories across generations and adapt their behavior to changing environments, all without a brain or nervous system. The research could shape how scientists think about bacterial infections and antibiotic treatment.

mix_items.title: The Commonwealth Games relay taking aim at ocean plastic
mix_items.summary: A new campaign is using sport’s global reach to stop one million pieces of plastic reaching seas and rivers The post The Commonwealth Games relay taking aim at ocean plastic appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
mix_items.summary: Europe’s rivers flowed more freely, scientists hailed a new pill for pancreatic cancer, and a ‘lost species' was found, plus more The post What went right this week: the good news that matters appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: My morning at a sober breakfast rave – and what I got out of it
mix_items.summary: A new ecstatic dance craze fuses spirituality, physicality and fun. Sarah LaBrecque joined 100 ravers one Sunday morning The post My morning at a sober breakfast rave – and what I got out of it appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden driver of aging — Simple supplement reversed brain decline
mix_items.summary: A newly identified brain protein may play a major role in how the body ages. Researchers discovered that declining levels of Menin in the hypothalamus triggered inflammation, memory problems, bone loss, and other aging-related changes in mice. Restoring Menin reversed several of these effects, while a simple amino acid supplement called D-serine boosted cognition. The discovery opens a surprising new path for fighting age-related decline.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: M-am culcat devreme. In sensul ca am stat devreme pe telefon.

satire_ro.title: Gabi Tamaş nu ştie în ce să investească cei 100.000 de euro de la Survivor: vin, bere sau tărie?
